title: Thoughts on Numbered Fleet Command
description: Among the senior mentors from whom I have learned the most in the Navy, most describe numbered fleet command as the best job they ever had.
text:
Among the senior mentors from whom I have learned the most in the Navy, most describe numbered fleet command as the best job they ever had. Despite my having served in Seventh Fleet as a subordinate commander twice before, commanding this “Team of Teams” surprised and challenged me every day. It was, quite simply, the best and hardest job I have ever had.
One day, a young leader reading this will rise to numbered fleet command, and, like command at sea, it is a job worth waiting for. In the spirit of helping those who follow do it better than I did, I pass along a few observations and best practices to help future commanders thrive in this extraordinary command opportunity.
Before You Report
Seek out those who have gone before. I was fortunate to learn from five previous numbered fleet commanders prior to reporting, and some of my best advice came from commanders from other regions. On a basic level, only those who have done the job have walked the same path you will and see the job most clearly for what it is—and is not.
Prepare for the marathon . . . and the sprint. While you need to take care of yourself, you are embarking on what likely will be a two-year journey in which you will need to be “all in.” Just as important, you will have to act decisively when needed. Starting the job well-rested and in decent shape will help.
Assess your stakeholders. Stakeholder analysis may feel like an academic exercise, but thanks to the advice of some valued mentors, I did it and benefited from it. Numbered fleet commanders must consider their sailors, ships, staff, boss, international counterparts, families, and, if forward deployed, host nations as stakeholders. You cannot please everyone, but it is helpful to understand the second- and third-order effects of your decisions from the perspective of your stakeholders.
Shore up personal, family, and medical readiness. Get everything you need taken care of before you report. I was fortunate to attend the Health Evaluation and Lifestyle Management (HELM) program prior to departing for Japan and would do it again if called to lead overseas again. Do everything you can to report in shape, healthy, and with your family well-situated for this upcoming adventure.
Communicate expectations early. The wider your span of control, the more deliberate you must be to ensure your priorities and values extend beyond your headquarters or flagship. Publish your command philosophy, visit your ships, and communicate with your force in multiple forums. If there are certain things you expect, such as topside appearance standards or for your commanders to maximize their time at sea, make those things clear.
Framing the Challenge
Focus on warfighting. As Chief of Naval Operations, former Seventh Fleet Commander Admiral Jonathan Greenert emphasized “Warfighting First” in his Navigation Plan. While you “Keep the Main Thing the Main Thing,” make sure you allocate time to keep your command and operational skills sharp. Amid all the activities, requirements, and well-intentioned programs that enable the force, ensuring your team of teams can fight and win must remain your central purpose.
Don’t forget the basics. As surface warfare officers, our job as commanders boils down to three things: “Lead, Drive, Fight.” Competencies such as navigation, maintenance, topside preservation, and training are also essential. What interests you will fascinate your subordinate commanders. As a numbered fleet commander with a wide span of control (in my case, seven task forces, 50 ships, and 27,000 sailors and Marines), you will need to think about how to keep the pulse on these fundamentals through commander’s update briefs, printed reports, or other readiness forums.
Relentlessly pursue readiness. Concepts are worthless if you cannot get your ships to sea or aircraft in the air. Tactical concepts and innovation are a grand part of our tradition and culture, but everything we do starts with ready crews, ships, and aircraft. Ensure your major commanders understand this basic fact and support them as they work to “make payroll” at pace and at scale.
Recognize everything will not go perfectly. While you will marvel at the number of things your team of teams gets right, your wide span of control means that, over time, some things will go awry. Mission shortfalls, material casualties, and liberty incidents all occurred on my watch, even as our sailors conducted thousands of engagements flawlessly during the same period. Think hard about how you will respond to those inevitable shortfalls without losing perspective or accepting a new, lower standard.
Remember that leaders cannot be victims. You own it. The great captains of history rarely believed they had enough time, resources, or people, but they pressed on. Like you, they were human beings leading in a complex and violent world with constrained resources and human frailties. Never walk by a problem. Move out to fix it.
Best Practices
Command through commanders, not staff. Your staff likely will be an incredibly effective team and conduit for your intent, but do everything you can to command through your commanders. They want to hear from you. Commander’s update briefs, one-on-one meetings, and an occasional commander’s conference are all tools to listen to, engage with, and coach your commanders.
Exercise your power to convene. Almost everything you do, and many of the most difficult aspects of the job, will require the support of external stakeholders. As a numbered fleet commander, your ability to convene a group to address and fix problems too big for a single command cannot be overlooked. Further, future convenings can be used to hold the collective team—including you—accountable to keep the momentum going.
Avoid leading by email. While email likely will remain a mainstay given the span of control and dispersed forces that come with the job, face-to-face meetings or picking up the phone can do wonders, conveying your intent in a fraction of the time it takes to draft an email.
Get to know your counterparts. Your fellow U.S. and allied fleet commanders not only will be excellent sounding boards, but also will be ready to help you with hard problems. Your international counterparts may use Line, Signal, or WhatsApp to begin unclassified conversations with you. Conversely, some nations still value the old customs, such as sending out holiday cards and letters.
Be present. While time is your most precious resource, nothing speaks louder than your physical presence. Work to visit every ship and all your task forces in person, multiple times if you can. If you have a flagship, deploy with it. Personal leadership at sea matters. You are never too busy to lead, engage, or go to sea.
Listen to the little voice inside your head. If it is telling you to step in, don’t wait another instant. Senior leaders can cut through the fog, signal intent, and get an organization moving in a way their subordinates may not be able to do. While leaders should be agents of stability, not chaos, you will not have the luxury to wait a year to make changes where needed.
Own your schedule, or it will own you. Your battle rhythm is your weapon system. There will be countless demands on your time, but you also will need time to think and engage. A note of caution: You will not make everyone happy, and commanders who fall prey to “institutional people pleasing,” as I did a time or two, will burn themselves out or risk diluting their focus.
Take care of your staff. Commanders work by, with, and through their staffs. Remember to value them. For those who are part of an afloat staff for the first time, embarking and debarking a flagship, major exercises, and meeting four-star questions in a timely manner may be new. As you tend to external stakeholders, make sure you are keeping your finger on the pulse of the team closest to you.
Never stop learning. How will you continue to make deposits in your intellectual bank account? If all you do is work without taking in different perspectives and ideas, intellectual depletion will surely follow. At sea, nothing beats reading a book to expand your horizons. If you are not a reader, audiobooks, YouTube, and podcasts provide other ways to, in the words of Stephen Covey, “sharpen the saw.”
Share this experience with your family. Wherever possible, include them in the journey. If you are with one of the forward deployed fleets, there will be incredible opportunities for your spouse to engage with host-nation members eager to meet you both. If you rely on your spouse for perspective, as I do, you will benefit immensely from having them share this journey with you.
Be confident and give confidence. In a world with many challenges, your job is to instill confidence in your team of teams. Make sure this confidence is hard-earned through training, repetition, and focus and is not just bluster. Your sailors will know the difference.
Commanding Seventh Fleet was the best and most challenging job I have ever had in a profession I love. For young leaders reading this, the job could one day be yours, and I hope these reflections make a difference in a job worth having.
